beats.text: A model in a training loop is a live Python object; deployment turns it into a self-contained, frozen artifact.
An artifact has three properties: it is portable, it is frozen so outputs are reproducible, and its static graph is optimizable.
The model passes through four stages: research, checkpoint, artifact, and a hardware-locked compiled engine.
Artifact size is the parameter count times the per-element width in bytes, so dtype decides where it fits.

visual.note: FP32 to 4, FP16 to 2, INT8 to 1, INT4 to 0.5 bytes per param

beats.text: A 7-billion-parameter model occupies 28 GB in FP32, 14 GB in FP16, 7 GB in INT8, and 3.5 GB in INT4.
Two techniques recover the graph from eager Python: tracing records one example run, scripting compiles the source.
ONNX is the Open Neural Network Exchange: a framework-agnostic graph format plus a versioned standard operator set.
An ONNX file is a serialized protobuf: nodes naming standard operators, the tensors between them, and weight initializers.
A hub collapses point-to-point conversion from F times R bespoke converters down to F plus R connectors.

visual.note: F frameworks, R runtimes, ONNX as the hub

beats.text: For 5 frameworks and 6 runtimes, 30 point-to-point integrations collapse to just 11 with ONNX in the middle.
ONNX Runtime dispatches one graph to pluggable execution providers: CUDA, TensorRT, OpenVINO, CoreML, or the browser.

visual.label: Execution providers from one ONNX graph

beats.text: ONNX is not free: dynamic control flow and custom ops can fail to export, and the opset version keeps moving.
Beside the neutral ONNX hub, each framework keeps a native artifact that often preserves more than ONNX can.
TorchScript captured PyTorch models for the libtorch C++ runtime, but torch.export is now the strategic direction.
SavedModel is TensorFlow's language-neutral serialization of graph, variables, and assets that TF Serving mounts.
TFLite, now LiteRT, converts a SavedModel into one compact FlatBuffer tuned for phones, microcontrollers, and NPUs.
Five formats span the field, from the cross-framework ONNX hub to the hardware-locked NVIDIA TensorRT engine.
Only ONNX clears the cross-framework filter; only edge formats survive the mobile filter, matching the decision tree.
A serving system loads the artifact, listens on a port, and turns request streams into prediction streams.

visual.sub: From passive file to running endpoint

beats.text: Throughput is batch size over batch time; with a large fixed cost and a small per-item cost, it climbs sharply.

visual.note: t_0 fixed per-call cost, beta marginal cost per item

beats.text: A GPU runs a batch of 32 almost as fast as a batch of 1, so dynamic batching is the key serving trick.
TorchServe is the simple PyTorch-only path; Triton is the framework-agnostic workhorse serving many backends at once.
Little's Law sizes a fleet: requests in flight equal the arrival rate times the mean time each request spends inside.

visual.note: L in flight, lambda arrival rate, W mean latency

beats.text: At 200 requests per second and a 0.5 second latency, 100 requests are always in flight and must be provisioned.
JAX is a NumPy-compatible library of composable function transformations that compile through the XLA backend.
Its transforms are orthogonal: jit lowers to XLA, grad takes the gradient, vmap batches, and they compose.

visual.note: jit(grad(vmap(f))) is one compiled batched gradient

beats.text: The price of composition is purity: transformed functions must be side-effect-free, and serving goes through StableHLO.
The ecosystem clusters around three poles: compilers and IRs, vendor engines, and weight formats.
Safetensors became the safe, zero-copy checkpoint format, while GGUF dominates the local consumer LLM world.

visual.label: Dominant weight formats: safetensors and GGUF

beats.text: The durable pattern is a funnel: train, capture the graph, compile to an engine, then serve behind a batching server.

visual.mark: each arrow trades generality for speed
